title: Sicilian ricotta cheesecake
subtitle: The Italian baked cheesecake — lighter, faintly orange-scented
excerpt: A baked cheesecake made with ricotta rather than cream cheese: lighter in texture, less rich, with a gentle flavour of candied orange peel and vanilla. No biscuit base, a short, sweet pastry crust from the tin base up the sides.
sourceNotes: Ricotta cheesecake (crostata di ricotta) is well documented in Sicilian cooking literature from the nineteenth century. Pellegrino Artusi, La Scienza in Cucina e l'Arte di Mangiar Bene (1891), includes ricotta-based pastry fillings that are the precursor of the modern Sicilian version. The orange-peel and vanilla scenting is characteristic of the Palermo tradition.

content.text: The New York cheesecake is dense and rich. This Sicilian version is lighter and finer. The ricotta gives a texture that is almost mousse-like at the centre. The pastry case is a

, a sweet Italian short pastry. It lines the base and sides of a springform tin. The filling is beaten ricotta, eggs, sugar, and the flavours that set Sicilian baking apart. Those are orange zest and candied peel, vanilla, and a scrape of lemon.

attrs.prepNote: drained in a fine sieve overnight if very wet

content.text: Rub the cold butter into the flour and icing sugar until the mixture resembles breadcrumbs. Add the egg yolks and lemon zest and work together until the dough just comes together, avoid overworking, which makes the pastry tough. Press into a disc, wrap, and refrigerate for 30 minutes.

Preheat the oven to 175°C conventional (155°C fan). Press two-thirds of the pastry into the base and up the sides of a greased 23 cm springform tin to a height of about 4 cm. Keep the remaining third for the lattice top if desired, or roll it thin and lay it over the filling as a solid lid. Refrigerate the lined tin for 15 minutes.

Beat the ricotta and sugar together until smooth. Add the eggs one at a time, then stir in the orange zest, orange juice, vanilla, and candied peel. Pour into the pastry-lined tin. If using a pastry top, lay strips in a lattice or lay the whole piece over the filling and seal the edges to the sides.

Bake for 45 to 55 minutes until the filling is set with just a faint wobble at the very centre, the pastry is pale gold, and the surface has coloured. The filling should not crack; if it does, the oven was too hot. Cool completely in the tin, then refrigerate for at least 4 hours. Dust generously with icing sugar before serving.
